text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and places the ports in a typical configuration using the XI robotic system, preparing for the robotic ascending colectomy.
The surgeon flips the omentum above the transverse colon, reflecting the transverse mesocolon to open up the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon flips the omentum above the transverse colon, reflecting the transverse mesocolon to open up the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon laparoscopically addresses adhesions obstructing the positioning of anatomical structures, carefully dissecting and removing the adhesions to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down adhesions that were hindering laparoscopic positioning.
The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to unfold the retroperitoneum after detaching the omental attachment.
The surgeon uses the tip-up grasper on arm number four to unfold the retroperitoneum after detaching the omental attachment.
The robotic grasper securely holds the transverse colon and pulls it towards the patient's left upper quadrant, repositioning the colon within the surgical field.
The robotic arm grasps the transverse colon and pulls it towards the patient's left upper quadrant, repositioning the colon within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon utilizes the third arm of the robotic system, specifically the tip-up fenestrated bipolar grasper, to manipulate tissues during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses the third arm, equipped with a tip-up fenestrated bipolar grasper, to grasp the mesocolon of the transverse colon and pull it towards the left upper quadrant, effectively unfolding and opening the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to grasp the mesocolon of the transverse colon, pulling it towards the patient's left upper quadrant to unfold and open the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon exposes the second portion of the duodenum, visible regardless of the patient's BMI, and identifies the ilicoid pedicle just inferior to it.

The surgeon uses a robotic instrument to hold up the ilicoid pedicle, stretching it towards the patient's right lower quadrant. The second portion of the duodenum is visible, located superior to the ilicoid pedicle.
The surgeon uses a grasper to hold up the ilicoid pedicle and the fold of Treves, stretching them towards the patient's right lower quadrant.
The surgeon points out anatomical landmarks, including the duodenum and the ilicoid pedicle, which is visible as a distinct swoop in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks, including the duodenum and the ilicoid pedicle, visible as a swoop. A reflection of light highlights a small sulcus in the peritoneum.
A reflection of light is visible in a small divot of the peritoneum.
The surgeon points out a small sulcus near the ilicoid pedicle, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection by making an incision in the small sulcus, initiating the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection by making an incision at the sulcus, a small groove in the peritoneum, using precise surgical instruments.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection using electrocautery on the cut setting, creating a clean incision in the peritoneum while minimizing bleeding.
The surgeon uses the cut setting on the cautery device to make a clean incision in the peritoneum, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to probe the retroperitoneal plane, gradually revealing the duodenum as it comes into view.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection in the retroperitoneal plane, gradually revealing the duodenum as it comes into view within one to two centimeters on the medial portion of the dissection, towards the patient's left.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the duodenum on the patient's left side as the procedure progresses.
The duodenum becomes visible in the surgical field as the surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon identifies the ovococcal pedicle and encircles it completely before dividing it.
The surgeon encircles the ovococcal pedicle and divides it, completing the dissection in the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon identifies the targeted structure and swiftly divides it, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon encircles the iliacolic pedicle, preparing to divide it during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon encircles the iliacolic pedicle, creating a window above it. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon seals and divides the vessel while ensuring the duodenum remains positioned below.
The surgeon creates a window above the iliacolic pedicle, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue to expose the area.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal and divide the iliacolic pedicle through the created window, ensuring the duodenum remains positioned downward.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to seal the vessel four times at the same spot before cutting, ensuring a secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal the vessel at the iliacolic pedicle, applying the device four times in the same spot before cutting.
The surgeon grasps the divided iliocolic pedicle and lifts it anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, demonstrating the manipulation of the pedicle after division.
The surgeon grasps the divided iliacolic pedicle and lifts it anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, demonstrating the manipulation of the pedicle post-division.
The surgeon lifts the divided iliacalc pedicle anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, revealing the surgical plane. The video shows the surgeon slightly deeper than intended in the dissection plane.

The surgeon lifts the divided iliacolic pedicle anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, revealing the surgical plane. The video shows the surgeon adjusting the depth of the dissection, lifting the plane upwards while brushing the deeper plane downwards.
The surgeon begins lifting the tissue plane upwards while brushing the adjacent plane downwards, manipulating the anatomical layers during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects towards the patient's right lower quadrant, peeling the cecum off the retroperitoneum. The dissection then turns northward towards the liver.

The surgeon dissects towards the patient's right lower quadrant, peeling the cecum off the retroperitoneum. The dissection then turns northward, peeling the ascending colon off, progressing towards the liver. The goal is to create a defect in the hepatococcal ligament, revealing the gallbladder and liver medially, indicating completion of the medial dissection.
The surgeon peels the cecum off the retroperitoneum and then proceeds to peel the ascending colon away from surrounding tissues, making a rightward turn in the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the liver, aiming to create a defect in the hepatococcal ligament for better visualization of the ligament.
The surgeon visually identifies the gallbladder and liver positioned medially within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the medial dissection, having successfully separated the cecum and ascending colon from the retroperitoneum and progressed towards the liver.
The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to assist with retraction while brushing down the tissue on the right side of the screen, enhancing the visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a brushing motion to gently separate tissue layers, likely in the area of the ascending colon or cecum based on previous dissection steps.
The surgeon utilizes a third surgical instrument to assist with retraction, enhancing the exposure of the surgical field.
The surgical view remains focused on the retroperitoneal space, showing ongoing dissection in this anatomical area.

The surgeon uses the XI robot to adjust the camera angle from thirty degrees down to thirty degrees up for a better view. The hepatic flexure is lifted with the left hand while the right hand pushes down, creating a visible defect in the hepatic colic ligament. The abdominal wall becomes visible in the background.
The surgeon uses their left hand to lift tissue while simultaneously pushing down with their right hand, manipulating the surgical field.
The surgeon uses their left hand to lift and their right hand to push down, creating a large defect in the hepatic colic ligament.
The surgeon adjusts the robotic camera from a thirty-degree downward angle to a thirty-degree upward angle to enhance the view of the surgical field during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon lifts the hepatic flexure with the left hand while pushing down with the right hand, revealing the abdominal wall in the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon visualizes the lateral edge of the liver while applying downward pressure with the right hand to continue distal dissection along the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon visualizes the lateral edge of the liver while pushing down with the right hand to continue distal dissection. The dissection progresses towards the patient's left, aiming to reach the middle colic artery for division, and further extends to the splenic flexure and into the lesser sac.

The surgeon begins the subtotal or total colectomy by pushing down on the right side, moving distally towards the patient's left, while manipulating the hepatic flexure and abdominal wall for better exposure.
The surgeon divides the middle colic artery and continues dissection distally towards the splenic flexure, entering the lesser sac and progressing towards the right side of the abdomen.
After completing the medial dissection, the surgeon shifts focus to the lateral dissection, preparing to manipulate and separate tissue along the lateral aspect of the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and shifts focus to the lateral dissection, starting from the hepatic flexure down to the cecum. The third robotic arm lifts the transverse colon and omentum, revealing unusual adhesions that are divided. The assistant pulls the hepatic flexure downward to expose the defect in the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon begins the dissection from the hepatic flexure, moving downward towards the cecum, focusing on the anatomical structures along the right side of the colon.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the surgical instrument to lift the omentum off the transverse colon, providing a clear view of the underlying structures.
The surgeon encounters unusual adhesions and proceeds to divide them, separating the adhesions from surrounding tissue.
The assistant pulls the hepatic flexure of the colon downward towards the patient's feet, facilitating further dissection and exposure of the area.
The surgeon exposes the defect created in the retroperitoneal plane, revealing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon attempts to identify the large defect created in the retroperitoneal dissection, moving anteriorly to assess the area for safe tissue removal. The defect is visible in the current frame.
The surgeon attempts to move anteriorly, likely manipulating the transverse colon and surrounding structures to progress in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the retroperitoneal area to identify the large defect created during dissection, assessing the surrounding tissue to determine safe areas for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon points out the defect created during the retroperitoneal dissection, which is visible in the current frame.
The surgeon inserts a vessel sealer into the identified defect created during retroperitoneal dissection, indicating that the area to the left is safe for further surgical manipulation.

The surgeon inserts the vessel sealer into the identified spot, ensuring the area to the left is safe for dissection. The vessel sealer is used to seal tissue where omentum is adhered. The surgeon then uses hook cautery to dissect medially along the white line of Toldt, mobilizing the right colon down to the cecum.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal the area where omentum is adhered, opting for this tool instead of scissors.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to incise immediately medial to the white line of Toldt, unfolding the right colon and mobilizing it down to the level of the cecum.

The surgeon continues the mobilization of the right colon, using surgical instruments to carefully separate and manipulate the tissue. The process involves advancing the dissection to complete the right colonic mobilization.
The surgeon continues the mobilization of the right colon, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues the mobilization of the right colon, progressing towards completing the dissection and freeing the colon from its attachments.
The surgeon addresses the final attachments of the terminal ileum and cecum in the right lower quadrant, with the appendix becoming visible in the pelvic region.
The surgeon detaches the final attachments of the terminal ileum and the cecum from the right lower quadrant, completing the mobilization of these structures.
The appendix becomes visible as the surgeon continues the mobilization of the right colon.

The surgeon's third arm, off-camera, is grasping the hepatic flexure and pulling it towards the patient's left upper quadrant to provide counter-traction and macro-retraction. The left hand, equipped with a bipolar instrument, actively retracts tissue as the procedure progresses, focusing on the final terminal ileal attachments.
The surgeon's third arm manipulates the hepatic flexure, pulling it towards the patient's left upper quadrant, although this action is currently off camera.
The surgeon identifies and addresses the final terminal ileal attachments, preparing to detach them from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the medial colonic section, takes the iliac vessels, frees the duodenum, and mobilizes the right colon.

The surgeon completes the medial colonic dissection, taking the iliac vessels and freeing the duodenum, followed by the mobilization of the right colon. The next step involves dividing the terminal ileum and transverse colon in preparation for the anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to divide the terminal ileum and transverse colon, setting the stage for the upcoming anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon rotates the entire colon laterally to prepare for the division of the terminal ileum.

The surgeon rotates the entire colon laterally to set up for the terminal ileal division. The divided ileococcal pedicle is fished out and grasped with the third arm, pulling it towards the patient's left shoulder.
The surgeon pushes the colon laterally and retrieves the divided ileococcal pedicle, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the surgical instrument to grasp the divided ileococcal pedicle and pulls it towards the patient's left shoulder.
The surgeon pulls the divided ileococcal pedicle towards the patient's left shoulder, creating a visible V-shaped configuration within the mesentery of the terminal ileum.

The surgeon uses the third arm to pull the mesentery of the terminal ileum towards the patient's left shoulder, creating a visible V-shape. After cleaning the camera, the vessel sealer is used to divide the terminal ileal mesentery with two burns, aiming towards the fold of Treves. The assistant grabs and tents up the fold of Treves.
The camera view reveals the formation of a V-shaped structure within the mesentery of the terminal ileum as the divided ileococcal pedicle is manipulated.
The surgeon uses the third arm to pull the divided iliacalc pedicle towards the patient's left shoulder, forming a visible V shape within the mesentery of the terminal ileum.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery of the terminal ileum, creating a visible separation in the tissue.
The surgeon performs two burns on the mesentery using a vessel sealer, visibly cauterizing the tissue.
The surgeon aims the instrument towards the fold of Treves, a visible anatomical landmark in the mesentery during the colectomy procedure.
The assistant grasps the fold of Treves and tents it upward, creating a visible elevation in the tissue.
The surgeon aligns the mesentery using the vessel sealer, ensuring precise positioning for further dissection.

The mesentery is aligned precisely, with the assistant pulling to create optimal tension. In a scenario involving a Crohn's patient, the surgeon adjusts the approach to take more mesentery and terminal ileum by moving the dissection more proximally and to the left.
The surgeon adjusts the approach to take more of the terminal ileum (Ti) by moving the dissection slightly more proximal and to the left, in response to the assistant's traction on the mesentery.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the surgical field while the assistant pulls up a portion of the bowel, aligning it for precise resection of the small bowel.

The surgeon adjusts the angle and instructs the assistant to pull the bowel upwards, aligning it for precise division of the small bowel. The division continues until the bowel is skeletonized down to the mesenteric border of the terminal ileum, using Firefly imaging for enhanced visualization.
The surgeon continues to divide the mesentery, progressively separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to divide the tissue, likely focusing on separating sections of the bowel or mesentery as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to divide the tissue, progressively skeletonizing it down to the mesenteric border of the terminal ileum.
The surgeon introduces a surgical stapler to divide the bowel, aligning it for precise resection.
The surgeon brings in a blue linear stapler to divide the terminal ileum and transverse colon, completing the division with a single load.
The surgeon prepares to use the stapler to divide the tissue, focusing on the mesenteric border of the terminal ileum.
The surgeon uses a blue stapler to divide the tissue, typically requiring a single load to complete the division.
The surgeon moves towards the patient's right upper quadrant, preparing for further surgical actions in this area.
The surgeon moves towards the patient's right upper quadrant to divide the colon just distal to the hepatic flexure, preparing to remove the right colon due to a cecal cancer.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the colon at the precise location intended for separation.
The surgeon prepares to divide the colon just distal to the hepatic flexure, marking the precise location for transection.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the surgical instrument to grasp the transverse colon, preparing for further manipulation or division.

The surgeon uses the third arm to grasp and elevate the transverse colon towards the patient's mid-epigastrum. The duodenum is visible and pushed down before dividing the mesentery of the transverse colon using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon elevates the transverse colon, preparing for division distal to the hepatic flexure as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon has elevated the transverse colon to the mid-epigastric region of the patient, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon exposes a portion of the duodenum adjacent to the transverse colon, which is grasped and elevated, preparing for division distal to the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon prepares to push down the transverse colon, which is currently grasped with the third arm of the surgical instrument.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery of the transverse colon, separating it from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery of the transverse mesocolon, navigating through the dense tissue and omentum. The robotic view magnifies the instrument, showing large bites being taken to separate the mesentery.
The surgeon divides the mesentery of the transverse colon, navigating through the dense mesenteric and omental tissue, which presents a challenging aspect of the operation.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to take large bites through the transverse mesocolon, progressively dividing the tissue.

The transverse colon is fully skeletonized, with the surgeon's third robotic arm holding the point of division. The stapler is then brought in and positioned to divide the colon, while maintaining the tinea libra visible.
The surgeon uses the robotic third arm to hold the transverse colon at the point of division, following complete skeletonization of the transverse colon.
The surgeon prepares to bring in a surgical stapler, positioning it to slide into place while maintaining alignment with the tinea libra of the colon.
The surgeon points to the tinea on the transverse colon, highlighting its location and appearance.
The surgeon positions the tinea libra within the crotch of the stapler, using multiple instruments to align the tissue precisely for stapling.

The surgeon aligns the tinea libra within the crotch of the stapler using various instruments for precise positioning. The Firefly imaging system is activated, revealing a lack of perfusion near the stapler. The stapler is then adjusted slightly to the patient's left, ensuring proper alignment and perfusion before proceeding.
The stapler is positioned in the mid portion of the surgical field, aligning with the tinea.
The surgeon observes that the tissue is not perfusing properly up to the location where the stapler is positioned in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the stapler and advances it slightly to the patient's left, positioning it more distally on the colon.
After completing the division of the colon, the specimen is positioned above the right level of the liver in preparation for the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the division of the colon distal to the hepatic flexure and parks the specimen above the right level of the liver, preparing the surgical field for the anastomosis.
